title: Produce Bin Wall Shelf
summary: A wall-hung basket-style shelf with 30°-angled sides and a gapped front edge for airflow — built from a handful of 1x6 and 1x3 offcuts, sized here as the larger of two published sizes.
description:
This shelf's whole shape comes from two angled side pieces rather than a box — cut the sides at 30° and everything else follows from there, since the front piece attaches with a deliberate gap left at the bottom for airflow. That gap is a real design decision, not a fit issue: produce needs air circulation to avoid rotting in a closed bin, which is the entire point of building this in wood slats rather than a solid container.

Build from the back forward: the back board attaches to the bottom first, nailed about every 4" for a joint that's carrying real produce weight over time, then the angled sides attach to both the bottom and the back. The front piece goes on last, leaving that airflow gap deliberately rather than closing the bin up.

**Locate a real wall stud before hanging** — this is a loaded shelf meant to hold fruit and vegetables, and 2-1/2" screws into drywall alone won't hold that weight reliably over time. This design publishes in two sizes; the cut list here is the larger bin, with the smaller size's numbers carried in the notes.

cutList.note: Cut at 30° angles. Smaller bin: 5-1/2" sides.
Smaller bin uses a 1x3 for the bottom/front pairing instead — see source for the swapped size.

steps.title: Attach the back and bottom
steps.body: Nail the back board to the bottom board with 1-1/4" brad nails and wood glue, nailing about every 4" for a joint that will carry real produce weight.

steps.title: Cut and attach the sides
steps.body: Cut the sides at 30° angles with a miter saw, circular saw, or jigsaw. Attach to the shelf bottom and back with 1-1/4" brad nails and wood glue.

steps.title: Attach the front
steps.body: Nail the front piece to the sides with 1-1/4" brad nails and wood glue, leaving a deliberate gap between the front and bottom for airflow.

steps.title: Hang on studs
steps.body: Locate real studs in the wall and hang the shelf to them with 2-1/2" self-tapping screws — a loaded produce shelf needs a real stud, not just drywall anchors.

images.alt: Wall-hung wooden produce bin shelf with angled sides
